title: Kuku sabzi
subtitle: Persian herb frittata with walnuts and barberries
excerpt: An egg-based Persian frittata made with a very large quantity of fresh herbs: parsley, coriander, dill, and chives, with walnuts and barberries folded through. The outside is dark brown and crunchy; the inside is densely herbed and almost green. Eaten at Persian New Year, served at room temperature as a starter or as part of a spread.
sourceNotes: Kuku sabzi (herb frittata) is one of the most important Persian dishes, traditionally eaten at Nowruz (Persian New Year) as a symbol of spring and new growth. It is documented in Margaret Shaida's 'The Legendary Cuisine of Persia' (1992) and Najmieh Batmanglij's 'Food of Life' (1986). The key is the ratio of herbs to egg, the herbs are the substance of the dish, and the egg is the binder.

content.text: The ratio of herb to egg in kuku sabzi is the reverse of most Western frittatas. Here there is an enormous amount of herb (approximately 200 g of fresh herbs) bound by only 5 to 6 eggs. The kuku should be almost solid with green herb, with the eggs providing just enough binding to hold it together. A kuku made with the proportions of an Italian frittata is a different (and lesser) dish.

The kuku is cooked in a cast-iron or ovenproof frying pan: the base is set on the hob, then the pan goes into a moderate oven to finish the top, or a lid is placed on to steam-finish it. The outside should be deep brown and slightly crispy. Serve cut into wedges, at room temperature, with bread and yoghurt.

Beat the {{eggs}} with the {{baking-powder}} and salt. Add all the chopped herbs ({{parsley-flat}}, {{coriander}}, {{dill}}, {{chives}}) and {{fenugreek-leaves}} if using. Fold in the {{walnuts}} and {{barberry}} if using. The mixture will look more herb than egg, this is correct. Heat the {{extra-virgin-olive-oil}} in an ovenproof frying pan over medium heat. Pour in the herb-egg mixture and spread to an even layer. Cook undisturbed for 5 to 6 minutes until the base is set. Transfer to a 180°C fan oven and bake for 15 minutes until just set through. Rest for 5 minutes before cutting into wedges.

items.fix: Squeeze the herbs in a cloth after chopping to remove excess moisture. Use a full 6 eggs for 200 g of herbs
items.cause: Too much herb moisture or egg quantity insufficient
items.symptom: Kuku is soggy in the middle

items.fix: The base needs medium heat, not high. High heat sets a thick crust before the centre is cooked. Moving it to the oven for the second stage is the most reliable method
items.cause: Hob heat too high at the start
items.symptom: Outside burns before the inside sets

items.fix: The herbs should feel excessive when mixing, about 200 g of chopped herb for 6 eggs is the correct ratio. If you have used less, add more next time
items.symptom: Kuku tastes predominantly of egg rather than herb

items.fix: Use a generous amount of oil and ensure it is fully hot before adding the mixture. A cast-iron or non-stick pan releases more reliably
items.symptom: Difficult to remove from the pan

content.text: Kuku sabzi is the Nowruz dish, made for Persian New Year at the spring equinox when the fresh herbs symbolise new growth and the renewal of the year. It is one of the seven symbolic foods of the Haft-sin table. The green of the herbs represents nature coming back to life after winter. Outside the Nowruz context, kuku sabzi is eaten throughout the year as a portable, room-temperature dish taken on picnics (another beloved Iranian tradition), served as part of a cold mezze, or packed for journeys. Its density and richness make it deeply satisfying despite being a simple egg dish.
